title: Deuteronomy 32:8 and the Sons of God
author: heiser

entries.title: The Textual Variant in Deuteronomy 32:8 — Sons of God vs Sons of Israel
entries.content_summary: The Masoretic Text of Deuteronomy 32:8 reads 'sons of Israel' (bny yśr'l), but the Septuagint reads 'angels of God' (angelōn theou) or 'sons of God' (huiōn theou), both presupposing a Hebrew Vorlage of either bny 'lhym or bny 'lym. Two Dead Sea Scrolls manuscripts (4QDeutj and 4QDeutq) confirm the 'sons of God' Hebrew reading. 4QDeutj clearly reads bny 'lwhym and 4QDeutq has spaces for additional letters after 'l, confirming this was not merely bny 'l.
entries.content_detail: The Table of Nations in Genesis 10-11 catalogs 70 nations but does not include Israel. The same Hebrew root (prd) used in Genesis 10:5 for 'separation' of nations appears in Deuteronomy 32:8 for dividing humankind, confirming Genesis 10-11 as the backdrop. The logical problem with the MT reading: How could the number of pagan nations be determined in relation to Israel (70 family members per Gen 46:27/Exo 1:5) when Israel did not yet exist at the time of Babel? Ugaritic mythology states that El Elyon (the Most High — the same title used in Deut 32:8) fathered 70 sons (KTU 1.4:VI.46), providing an exact linguistic parallel with the Hebrew bny 'lym / bny 'lhym.

entries.title: Text-Critical Methodology: No A Priori MT Superiority
entries.content_summary: No text tradition should be assigned a priori superiority. The MT rose to prominence not by intrinsic textual factors but by political and socioreligious developments. Evidence from Qumran testifies to certifiable textual plurality among Jews from the 3rd century BC to the 1st century AD. Three independent text traditions (proto-MT, LXX Vorlage, Samaritan Pentateuch) coexisted at Qumran without suppression. The LXX was abandoned by Jews only after Christianity adopted it, not because it was inferior.
entries.content_detail: The MT of Samuel suffers significant haplography. Kings has extensive pluses, minuses, and transpositions. MT Ezekiel chapters 1 and 10 are riddled with textual corruptions. MT Jeremiah is one-sixth longer than LXX Jeremiah, demonstrating expansionistic tendencies. The Qumran fragments supporting the LXX 'sons of God' reading (4QDeutj,n) are among 'unaligned texts' that show equal agreement and disagreement with all three major textual traditions.

entries.title: Parablepsis Cannot Explain the Variant in Deuteronomy 32:8
entries.content_summary: The proposal that a scribe accidentally omitted 'yśr'l' from 'bny yśr'l' by parablepsis (leaving 'bny 'l') fails because: (1) 4QDeutj clearly reads bny 'lwhym, not bny 'l — the extra consonants hwym cannot be explained by parablepsis; (2) 4QDeutq has spaces for additional letters after 'l, also ruling out a simple 'bny 'l' reading. Advocates would have to argue both parablepsis AND deliberate scribal insertion of extra letters — an extremely unlikely two-step corruption.
entries.content_detail: More critically, there exists ANOTHER textual variant in Deuteronomy 32:43 where divine beings (sons of 'lhym/'lym) are also the focus. In Deut 32:43, the MT demonstrably lacks a complete bicolon — the missing colon is exactly the one referencing divine beings in the Qumran and LXX texts. This 'parallel corruption' means defenders of MT priority must argue for TWO accidental changes in the same chapter that both happen to eliminate references to divine beings. It is far more likely both texts were intentionally altered in MT to eliminate references to divine beings deemed polytheistic.

entries.title: The Divine Council in 1 Kings 22:19-23
entries.chapter_or_episode: The Divine Council in the Old Testament
entries.content_summary: 1 Kings 22:19-23 depicts Yahweh enthroned among the members of His council ('all the host of heaven') who stand before Him — a technical term paralleled in Ugaritic council scenes. Yahweh asks a question in a form paralleled in Ugaritic literature. A spirit volunteers to be a lying spirit in the mouths of Ahab's prophets. Yahweh approves the course of action He knows will succeed, and the messenger is commissioned. The council does not exercise actual authority but serves to 'reemphasize and execute His decisions.'
entries.content_detail: This scene resembles Ugaritic council scenes where the chief deity El sits enthroned and addresses the assembly. The prophet Micaiah was permitted to observe the deliberations of the divine 'boardroom meeting' and thus pronounce with certainty 'thus saith the LORD' as a messenger of the divine assembly. Visions of Yahweh's divine council authenticated the prophet's message — a sort of test of true 'propheticity.'

entries.title: Psalm 82 — Yahweh Judges the Divine Council Members
entries.content_summary: Psalm 82 depicts God (Elohim) standing in the assembly of El (adat-'el) judging in the midst of the gods (elohim). The elohim of v.1b and 6a are divine beings, not human judges. Key evidence: (1) They are called 'sons of Elyon' (bny 'elyon) — Elyon is a completely transparent title for deity in both Hebrew and Ugaritic, used only of God/El, never of humans. (2) The chiastic structure of v.1 compels understanding the second elohim as divine. (3) The particle 'aken' in v.7 creates a strong antithetical contrast with v.6 — 'I had thought [you were gods]... but you shall die like Adam.' If they were already mortals, telling them they would die like mortals is nonsensical.
entries.content_detail: Cyrus Gordon demonstrated that translating elohim as 'rulers' or 'judges' lacks validity and is an example of theologically 'protecting' God. The terms and themes are present in Ugaritic literature — Elyon, princes, gods all appear in the Ugaritic poem 'the Gracious Gods.' The notion of the elohim 'falling' like 'one of the Shining Ones' (ha-sarim) is found in Canaanite mythology about the fall of one of the 'bn srm' ('sons of the shining ones'). The Akkadian cognate saruru means 'shining' (used to describe Venus in astronomical texts). This connects to Isaiah 14:12-15 (Lucifer/Shining One) and Ezekiel 28:12-17 (the anointed guardian cherub). Psalm 82:7 says the divine beings will die like Adam and fall like the Shining One — stripped of immortality and cast from their high estate.

entries.title: Divine Council Terminology: Ugaritic and Hebrew Bible Comparison
entries.content_summary: Precise linguistic parallels exist between Ugaritic divine council terminology and the Hebrew Bible. For the assembly: Ugaritic 'dt 'ilm ('assembly of the gods') = Hebrew 'adat 'el (Psalm 82:1). Ugaritic sd = Hebrew sod yhwh ('assembly of Yahweh', Jer 23:18) / sod 'eloah (Job 15:8). For members: Ugaritic 'ilm ('gods') = Hebrew elohim/elim. Ugaritic bn 'il ('sons of El') = Hebrew bene elim/bene elohim (Psalm 29:1; 89:6; Gen 6:2,4; Job 1:6; 2:1). Ugaritic phr kkbm ('congregation of stars') = Hebrew kokebe boqer ('morning stars', Job 38:7) / kokebe 'el ('stars of God', Isa 14:13). For meeting place: Ugaritic Mount Sapanu = Hebrew yarkete saphon ('heights of the north', Psalm 48:1-2); Ugaritic phr m'd ('assembled congregation') = Hebrew har mo'ed ('mount of assembly', Isa 14:13).
entries.content_detail: Critically, the Hebrew Bible maintains a hierarchy within the council that parallels Ugaritic: the elohim/elim (gods/divine beings) are not the same as the mal'akim (messengers/angels). At no point are the mal'akim ever designated as elohim, elim, or bene elohim in the Hebrew text. English Bible translations that render all these terms as 'angels' blur the distinctions and obscure the divine council. The members of the council had higher status than the messenger beings. The 'hosts' of Yahweh (tseba'ot) is an umbrella term including all categories of nonhuman beings.

entries.title: Jesus' Use of Psalm 82:6 in John 10:34 — A Claim to Deity Via the Divine Council
entries.content_summary: The traditional interpretation that Jesus quotes Psalm 82:6 to say 'even human judges were called gods, so I can call myself Son of God' actually WEAKENS Jesus' claim to deity. If the elohim were just humans with a title, then Jesus is merely claiming a non-ontological title. This is no more an argument for deity than calling Moses elohim in Exodus 4:16/7:1. Instead, Jesus was reminding his Jewish adversaries of the OTHER divine beings — the elohim — in Yahweh's divine council. Their own Scripture affirmed multiple elohim. Jesus was saying: 'My Father has many divine sons (Psalm 82:6 testifies to this), yet I am unique — I am the monogenes son of God, qualitatively different from all other divine beings.' Interpreting Psalm 82:6 as human judges ROBS Jesus of a very pointed claim to deity.
entries.content_detail: The 'word of God' in John 10:35 is not a reference to receiving the Mosaic law (there is no reference to the law in all of Psalm 82). It refers to the judgment rendered against the divine beings by Yahweh in Psalm 82:7. Moses is called elohim in Exodus 4:16 and 7:1 not because he is a human 'judge' but because he functions as a member of God's council — he regularly spoke to Yahweh face to face and was a governing mediator ruling Israel at God's behest.

entries.title: The Nations Given to Fallen Divine Beings at Babel — Deuteronomy 32:8-9 Theology
entries.chapter_or_episode: The Nations Given Up to Fallen Divine Beings
entries.content_summary: Deuteronomy 32:9 says 'the LORD's portion is his people, Jacob his allotted inheritance.' The parallelism requires that if Israel was TAKEN (hlq) as Yahweh's allotted inheritance (nhlh), then the nations of v.8 were also GIVEN as an inheritance — to the sons of God. After observing humanity's rebellion before the flood and again at Babel, God 'gave humanity up' (cf. Romans 1) to lesser divine beings and created Israel ex nihilo as His own nation, beginning with Abraham in the very next chapter (Genesis 12). Each pagan nation was overseen by a divine being inferior to Yahweh. Deuteronomy 4:19-20 confirms this was punitive — God allotted the gods (the host of heaven) to the nations as punishment.
entries.content_detail: Tigay: 'These passages seem to reflect a biblical view that as punishment for man's repeated spurning of His authority in primordial times (Gen 3-11), God deprived mankind at large of true knowledge of Himself and ordained that it should worship idols and subordinate celestial beings. He selected Abraham and his descendants as the objects of His personal attention to create a model nation.' The verb hlq ('allotted') appears in both Deut 4:19 and 32:8, confirming the connection. This theology explains the entire biblical narrative: God disinherits the nations at Babel, gives them to the sons of God, takes Israel as his own, and the rest of Scripture traces the plan to reclaim the nations.

entries.title: Israelite Monotheism and the Reality of Plural Elohim
entries.chapter_or_episode: The Divine Council and Israelite Monotheism
entries.content_summary: If other divine beings do not actually exist, texts like Psalm 89:6-7 ('Who in the skies can compare with Yahweh? Who is like Yahweh among the sons of the gods? In the council of the holy ones God is greatly feared') and Psalm 29:1 ('Ascribe to Yahweh, O sons of the gods') would be eviscerated of meaning. The solution: Western Christians define 'GOD' as omnipotent, self-existent, omniscient, omnipresent — and assume any entity called elohim must have these attributes. But the ancient Semitic mind distinguished between Deity (God — possessing all these attributes uniquely) and divinity (god-likeness — sharing the category of 'divine being' without sharing the unique attributes). Yahweh is an elohim, but no other elohim is Yahweh.
entries.content_detail: The Hebrew Bible's divine council differs from pagan pantheons in critical ways: (1) Yahweh alone is Creator, unassisted — no divine being helped; (2) There is NO theogony — Yahweh produced the elohim without a consort; (3) No genuine rivals — Yahweh need not battle divine beings to maintain leadership. Isaiah simultaneously affirmed the existence of other divine beings AND the one true deity (Isa 40:25-26 — 'To whom will you compare me?' then 'He brings out the starry host one by one and calls them by name' — He created the other elohim and they line up at His command). It would be nonsensical to have Yahweh claim to command entities that don't exist. Genesis 1:26 'let us make man in our image' is a decision announced to the council, but the following verb 'so God created' is singular — only Yahweh did the creating.
